sentences.text: My name is-- last name is Kohn, K-- K-O-H-N.
First name is Zev, Z-E-V.
Yes.
Polish, I had a nickname, nickname of Yesha.--
Close to 66.
In Warsaw, Poland.
Well, my father was physician.
We lived in a big apartment on Targowa Street, which was a main thoroughfare on the part-- east part of Warsaw.
And, and I had one sister who is 10 years older than I am.
And my father had his office in the apartment.
And at the age of six, I went to school.
Samuel was the name of my father and Helena, my mother, and Miriam, my sister.
We were not observant.
My father was going to synagogue, but I don't remember going with my father to synagogue in my childhood in Warsaw.
We-- I would call we were assimilated.
We're talking Polish at home.
I don't remem-- there was Seder.
But I don't remember the-- I know there were matzos.
This I do remember.
Yeah.
Yeah, there was a big Jewish community.
And my father was one of the leaders of the Jewish community.
I don't know.
I was too little.
I didn't come into contact with the community.
Should I continue on my own now?
OK.
At the age of six, I went to school, to primary school, which was about three, four blocks away, maybe five blocks away.
It was a Polish school.
That is to say that the students and faculty of the school were Poles.
And I was maybe the only-- certainly, the only Jew in the class.
And maybe even the only Jew in the primary school.
And my first recollections are of being abused both, both by teachers and students all the time, particularly during the breaks.
And when I complained, I was-- there was nobody to complain to.
But I was coming home crying.
And so usually kids started, I think, school at seven.
I started at six.
By the end of this first year in school, they took me out from that school.
And I had to travel by tramway to the west side of Warsaw to a Jewish school, where also the spoken language was Polish.
But both the faculty and the students were all Jews.
And I was in that school for two years before the war broke out.
That is to say, I finished the third grade.
And we studied Hebrew as a second language.
And it was a very, so to speak, private school.
It took a long time to get there.
But I felt very good.
And that's basically my memories before the school-- I mean, before the out-- outbreak of the war.
Did I have non-Jewish friends in Warsaw?
No, I did not.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I had few friends, closer friends, in, in, in the, in the school I was going to.
And-- but they were all Jews.
Then the war broke out on September 1, 1939.
And we were then in Warsaw.
And the bombing of Warsaw started.
And there was some perception at that time that there is more bombing going on the east side of Warsaw than west.
And we went to the-- to some part of my, our family.
They were west side of Warsaw.
And we spent the remaining three to four weeks of whatever the siege of Warsaw in 1939 took time.
And we were usually bombarded through the daytime by bombers and through the night by artillery.
Several houses around the house where we were-- were living in the, in the basement-- collapsed.
And people-- and hunger started.
People were eating horses, dead horses, killed horses, from the street.
[PAUSES FOR 22 SECONDS] Anyway, when the bombardment ended up, we went back to our apartment in Targowa-- on Targowa, which is, as I said, where we lived on the one flight up.
And the Germans walked into Warsaw within a couple of days or the same day that we got there.
I remember looking through the window, bedroom window of my parents, and I saw the first-- [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] the first detachments of the German army come into Warsaw.
And there were small detachments.
Of course, at-- in the evening, there was curfew.
We were well-stocked with provisions, with food, because my father, as a Polish officer, was drafted few days before the war broke out.
He said-- he left in the middle of the night-- partly this was the routine then-- in his already, the way I remember, military uniform.
And, and he left.
So during the siege of Warsaw, my father wasn't there.
I was with my sister and, and my mother.
Now, when the German walked into Warsaw, there was a curfew.
And I remember my mother hollering every time I would make a terrible mistake and put the light on in the, in the bedroom.
Because the Germans were shooting already into the windows wherever they would see a light.
But then apparently in November sometime, there is a Polish day of independence.
And the way my, my memory tells me, the Germans, I remember, came to our apartment to look for my father.
And my understanding was that they were taking hostages, because they didn't want, so to speak, any disturbance during the Polish day of independence.
As it is, of course, my father wasn't then in Warsaw.
He, by that time, was on the eastern part of Poland with my aunt, which was already at that time occupied by, by Russians.
So they looked for my father in the bedroom.
And then something moved under the quilt.
And it was my chihuahua, my little doggy.
And first they saw that somebody's hiding there.
But then they realized there was a dog.
So anyway, they walked away.
And-- but at that time, my understanding is that my uncle in, in Lodz was taken as hostage.
And he was shot.
And in other words, he-- they, they were shooting these hostages.
So it was luck that my father wasn't already there in Warsaw.
From the understanding gained after the war, the way the statement by my father goes, he understood what was coming.
And he promised himself-- or promised my mother, too-- that the Germans would never take him alive.
So he apparently comprehended the dangers.
And that's why he wasn't there.
Anyway, he was then, as I mentioned before, in the eastern Poland when the Russians walked in.
And they were sorting out, when the Russians came, between the officers and the enlisted men.
My father was an officer.
He was clever enough to, to realize that when the Russians are dividing between the officers and non-officers, there will be no, no good coming out of this, particularly to the Polish officers.
He was one of the few, if I may say from my understanding now, Jews that were in-- were officers in Polish army.
Anyway, he quit, then, that group.
And he left and then came to my aunt in Rudna.
The reason why I'm mentioning it, because subsequently, he called for us from Rudna to come out of Warsaw and join him in Rudna.
In the meantime, just as a side remark, the, the Polish soldiers that were then herded together by the Russians were subsequently, the officers, were, were killed in Katyn Forest.
So it, it was first sort of a miracle that--not that miracle, but his enterprising spirit that he left that group.
Now, back to us in Warsaw, the messenger came from Pa-- from my father saying, join me in Rudna.
By that time, the Jews couldn't travel already on the trains.
So the idea was how to get the tickets?
And my fa-- my mother found a Polish woman.
She-- my mother-- gave her enough money to buy tickets for us by train to, to, to the border between, then, Germany and Russia.
And so the woman bought the tickets for herself, for us.
And also, my mother was enterprising enough that she gave her more money.
And one wounded Polish soldier was there.
This is to make, to make the group bigger, so we, as Jews, won't be as conspicuous as standing out in, in the group.
And that was well, well-thought-out, too.
We-- I remember the day we packed up and were going to do the railroad station to-- of course, in cattle trains to go to the border.
I remember I was standing with my mother next to the-- how do you call these-- heating stoves, big, tile stoves.
And at that time, I was nine years old.
And I told my mother that I have a feeling that-- as we are leaving the apartment-- that we'll never come back.
And my mother says, shush, don't talk like this.
But that's exactly how it was.
And I'm sure my mother knew, also, that chances are, we'll never come back.
Anyway, we got to the train.
And on the train, in one of these lesser sta-stations, they came.
The t came, or whatever it was, Gestapo or whoever, with a Pole, looking for the Jews and to throw them off the moving train.
Because the Jews were not allowed to, to, to travel by train.
And they came.
And I remember that also quite distinctly.
And the Pole took liking to my sister.
She was at that time 19 years old.
And he told her in Polish, don't worry.
I, I won't give you away to the Germans.
Because you know, giving to the Germans, it means to be thrown out from the moving train, which wasn't exactly in our schedule, so to speak.
So we get out of the train.
And there are Germans there and one officer, who was hollering in German to get out of the train.
And there was a cart to put us in the cart.
And they took us to some German station.
And they transported us.
They gave us a lift to, to the, to the border itself.
I also remember-- and that may not be 100% accurate-- that that officer came-- my mother was fluent in German-- came to my mother and says, and said to her that he's not going to harm us.
And mind you, that was already time when we, as Jews, would walk the streets of Warsaw, the Polish hoodlums would stand there and finger us out and say, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
In other words, the Poles knew exactly how to deal with the Jews already then.
The Germans, apparently in this case, didn't have the program yet of-- with the Jews, even though they were roughing up the Jews on the streets already, too.
And there were-- there was a hearsay that the ghetto would be formed soon.
But historically, the ghettos actually formed much later.
Now, so they dropped us someplace close to, to the border.
Apparently, they said the officer suspected that we are Jews but didn't-- no harm came to us.
So all this bunch of refugees-- a, a wounded Polish soldier, the Polish woman, and such-- they dropped us a few miles away from the actual no man's land, from the border.
And there was a big group.
We're crossing near Malkinia, which was the crossing point between Germany and Russia.
And there was a big bunch of people as walking as refugees walk, past these cottages of peasants.
And my mother says to, to us, there's too many of us in one group.
We cannot risk this.
Because if they catch us, then the, the whole thing will, will be a big fiasco.
So the first cottage was on the side.
She started talking to these peasants.
And so we broke away from the main group.
We stayed with the, the cottage of the, of the peasants.
And they said, yes, they will give us a cart.
And at night, they would put us across the border.
They were Ukrainian peasants.
And so we stayed there, this particular group-- the wounded soldier, the Polish woman, and us.
And night came.
They put us on the cart.
And we're driving through the woods.
But prior to that, we noticed that there were some conversations between them.
And some of the men from this household left the cottage first.
Well, sure enough, in the middle of the night, in the middle of nowhere in the woods, we were held up by these people.
Obviously, the drivers, the people that drove the cart, were in cahoots with the, with the people that put guns to us.
And they made it very simple.
They said, you give us everything you have on you.
And if we find anything that you didn't give us, then we will shoot the person that has it.
So my sister had a $100 bill in, in the curls of her hair.
And she forgot she had it there.
But my mother reminded her.
And then, I think my mother had two watches on her arm.
She gave everything to, to these robbers, Ukrainian robbers, and that then let, let us go.
And the cart left us right there with the, with the loot.
And a few steps farther, we continued on in the middle of the night on some mud road.
And there was a-- how do you call it-- the, the post.
How do you call it-- the thing that goes up and down?
Some, some kind of guard?
I don't know that English.
I don't know, I don't know myself.
Anyway, so we realized that that must be-- we must be getting someplace close to the border.
So we passed it in order to leave German, German soil.
And there was a little, a little pine forest.
And we all slept there.
In other words, at least I slept.
My mother let me put her head in her lap.
And I slept.
With the first light, early, very early, we got up from the forest.
And we walked in opposite direction from where we came.
And sure enough, within few-- it, it transpired that we spent the night in no man's land.

Within few hundred feet, somebody walks out from behind a bush with a gun in his hand, obviously a Russian soldier, and says to us in Russian-- my mother knew Russian, too-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which means, turn around and go back where you came from.
Because the Russians, whatever the Germans were letting the refugees out, the Germans were not-- the Russians were not letting in.
So of course, everybody started crying.
And it probably broke the heart of the Russian soldiers.
He says, OK-- he saw a bunch of kids and wounded soldier and, and, and women, and took us to the station there, right by the border.
Well, by the border, that put us overnight.
Sure enough, there was one doctor there.
My mother came to him.
And she told him that my father is a physician.
Turn out that the guy was a Jewish doctor.
And it-- he gave us big hunks of sugar to eat.
And somehow he arranged that the next morning-- we still someplace spent few hours in a cottage of peasants there, slept in one bed, the, the whole family.
And, and there was no floor in the, in these cottages.
There was only mud.
And anyway, we, we went-- we dried up a little bit.
First thing next morning, we took the train.
And we went to Rudna.
And we joined my father, my aunt, and my two cousins.
This is the Weinberg family, Franka.
And so the-- so-to-speak, there we are.
And that was, then, the very end of 1939.
Now that takes us to a segment of time between 1939, end of '39, to June, 1941, when the Germans invade Russia.

And so, now I can come to, now I can come to, to the end of this chapter, out of [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] out of Warsaw, the German beginning of it, into Russia, and the next two years in Russia until the breakout of '41 war.
And I will stop here, I think.
I think the tape is ending here now.
We have few more minutes.
OK, now, so-- so then we come to my aunt.
And already, my other aunt with her two children is there, Kraushar, with her two boys and her husband, also a physician.
And initially, all these three sisters with a whole bunch of kids are in-- were in one apartment.
Within few months, also my uncle, Shimon, from the-- my father's side came.
And Jerrzyk, my cousin from my paternal uncle, Daniel, came there.
Also Wladek came, the son of my maternal sister, Basia.
And now all these people came because they were young, and they could walk.
So they walked, whatever it took, to cross the border.
And, and the whole mishpokhe was in one place.
And then I went to school, to Russian school.
I learned Russian.
And my father was working.
And nobody lived in one-- in complete peace of mind these two years preceding the war.
Actually, it's a year and a half or so, where anxiety the Germans were still-- may still go farther.
That was worry number one.
And the main worry was that the Russians were deport all these people that crossed the border into Siberia.
And several members of our family were deported into Siberia.
That was discussed, should we go inside Russia, deportation or otherwise, or should we say in eastern Poland and risk the second appearance of, of Germans?
That's it?
Yes?
We're still going.
We're still going.
So, how about I finish here, OK?
Because I have to--
